## [00:00 - 03:00]

Okay, now I want to talk about building early buzz. This is getting people excited and talking about what you're going to be doing even before you go into pre-launch. You can even do the testimonial cruncher and get some great testimonials ahead of time. But the real important deal with the shot across the bow is that it lets people know something's coming before you're actually in pre-launch before. I mean, when there's no hint of a pitch, you tell people something's coming and you're excited about it. If you go back and look at the email that I wrote, it's like, boy, we've been working this thing for years. We're so excited. Can you just help us out by taking this survey? So it wasn't like, hey, we've been working this thing for years, boy, we're really excited and please buy it, buy it, buy it, buy it. No, it was actually, it's like you're letting people in on a secret that you're so excited about. And so the big thing with the shot across the bow, and I mean, I don't know, you get great feedback, you can do the testimonial cruncher, but the biggest failure I think is in generating some buzz. So let's just talk a little bit about the characteristics of buzz. And now every market is different. And in some markets, they're more email-centric, some markets might be more forum-centric, some might be more live event-centric or social media or whatever. Every market's different. I mentioned this in the introductory video, in the introductory pre-launch video that the internet marketing world where I currently sell product, one of the markets that I sell product into, very, very connected. Everyone's on everyone's lists. People go to forums. People all do social media. They're all on Twitter or Facebook. It's a super connected market. So I need to hopefully take advantage of that, but take that into account when I am doing my pre-prelaunch. So buzz travels most easily through channels that have trust and authenticity. People don't go and start talking about you and doing word of mouth for you if they think you're just advertising, if they think you're just selling something. The buzz about something is when people are genuinely excited about it. What I'm talking about here is like a lot of social media, a lot of forums. And generally, if people, they can see through it if you try to game it for the most part.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

So you have to be authentic. The more authentic you are and the more you communicate in these authentic channels that have trust, the quicker the buzz will spread. I like to hit multiple modalities. So I like to maybe let people know something's coming. Maybe I'm interviewed somewhere. Maybe I drop a hint in an email. Maybe I do a little social media, but you like to use multiple modalities in media. And a little mystery really does go a long way. So a lot of times I'll try to structure it. So I'm dropping breadcrumbs here and there and people can try to piece it together. It's almost like a scavenger hunt. You let some people know something's coming in one area and then you start talking maybe a little bit about the product in another area. People will feel great about connecting those two things or those three things. And now they've got a secret, right? And the thing about a secret is no one likes to keep a secret. So if you can string the breadcrumbs together, you have a little mystery. It can actually go a long ways towards getting the buzz moving along. I like to think about mavens and connectors. Malcolm Gladwell talked about them in his book, The Tipping Point. Mavens are people that just love to collect data and information about a, whatever, a topic. Connectors are people that know lots and lots of people, especially lots of people that move in different arenas. A lot of the people you see on social media, they have very big followings are mavens and connectors. And then I just think about persons of influence within your market or niche. And those are the people that I wanna reach and get them talking. So a person of influence these days is mostly a publisher. Someone they might publish via email or on a blog or social media or whatever. Always think about your raving fans and your champions and how you can get them involved in your pre-pry launch. And I just mentioned this, leaving breadcrumbs, basically leaving clues to a mystery. People love that scarce information, right? And if they have the scarce information, they just wanna tell other people about it. So let's look at some examples. Now, one of the things I'm lucky enough to do is count on my joint venture partners and my affiliates to create buzz. It's almost like they're paid buzz creators. And what they'll do is they'll, when I've got a new version of pre-pry launch formula coming out, they will go out into the market and they'll start blogging about it and they'll start putting up links about it. Maybe even mention it in social media. And they'll try to get people excited. Of course, they're looking for an affiliate commission. So one of the things I did with pre-pry launch formula three is basically start this sort of JV buzz campaign way in advance.

## [06:02 - 09:02]

So this is an email I sent to my JVs. This was literally sent out a month before I started pre-launch. So we're talking probably six or seven weeks before we actually opened our cart for the product launch. And so this, I'm just letting them know something's coming. Just a quick heads up, I've got an all new version of product launch formula coming soon. This will be product launch formula three. And then I just go on to give them some details. Pre-launch starts, launch starts here. So no trickery, no crazy exciting ninja trick here. I'm just basically, I know my partners will start blogging. They will start getting the word out if I give them a heads up. So basically I put this out a month ahead of time. And then I sent another one a week later, another one a week later. So it's basically every week. And then coming down into the final week, I sent them three or four emails. So by the time we went into pre-launch, I probably sent them five, maybe six emails sort of getting them pumped up. And then a lot of them are persons of influence. Or they are mavens or they're connectors. They've got social media followings. They got their publishers. They've got email lists. And so they're my paid buzz creators. And I just want to get them going as far ahead of time. So I sort of try to amp them up. You've probably already been through this with me a few times. ## [12:05 - 12:32]

People that travel to events are often connectors and persons of influence. So by putting myself at an event with four or 500 attendees many of them persons of influence, by doing a couple of interviews and by getting my JV partners going and by getting my list going with that shout across the bow, all of a sudden, even weeks before pre-launch, I had people anticipating what was coming.
